tagged_story_for_tts:
Small stars fade quietly. Massive stars explode violently.

The phrase "how stars die" hides a cosmic rule: their death matches their weight. A star’s end isn’t random—it’s written in its birth mass, like a clock ticking since day one.

Lightweight stars (up to eight times our Sun) die as white dwarfs. They puff into red giants first, shedding outer layers like a snake shedding skin. What remains is a Earth-sized ember—dense, dim, cooling for trillions of years. No drama. Just a slow goodbye.

Heavy stars (ten+ times our Sun) detonate as supernovae. Their cores collapse in seconds, crushing atoms into neutrons or quarks. The rebound tears the star apart in a flash brighter than a galaxy. If the core survives, it becomes a neutron star—a city-sized ball so dense a sugar-cube of it weighs as much as a mountain. Or, if the core is too heavy, it vanishes into a black hole, a pit where space itself folds inward.

Between these extremes? Unstable medium stars. They erupt in partial supernovae, losing layers but leaving a zombie core—a neutron star wrapped in a ghostly nebula.

Three related subjects are black hole birth, neutron star collisions, and how dust forms planets.
